metric_name: Instrument type
metric_value: Telephone survey questionnaire — version 2; refined and streamlined from v1
notes: Second version of the RAMP phone survey; Part One has 17 numbered questions (Q1-Q17 with some gaps) plus Part Two demographics. Key refinements: per-OS storage breakout replaces single total; 1-7 Likert scale replaces binary choice; terminology battery expanded from 3 to 5 terms.

metric_name: OS platform — direct responsibility probe
metric_value: Q1: Which OSes does your organization use AND which are you directly responsible for? (Mainframe / Windows / Unix-Linux)
notes: V2 improvement: asking both organizational use AND personal responsibility screens for the authoritative respondent for each OS type. This ensures storage data for mainframe is provided by the mainframe team, not a Windows admin guessing. Methodological refinement over v1's single OS breakout question.

metric_name: Per-OS storage architecture breakout — v2 design
metric_value: Q4.1-Q4.3: Per-OS capacity share / DAS-SAN-NAS mix / 12-month growth / max fill rate — separately for mainframe / Windows / Unix
notes: Major v2 enhancement: breaking out storage architecture by OS (Q4.1 Mainframe / Q4.2 Windows / Q4.3 Unix/Linux) rather than asking for a single total. This reveals that mainframe, Windows, and Unix shops had dramatically different architecture mixes (mainframe=internal ESCON/FICON; Unix=SAN; Windows=DAS/NAS). This granularity was essential for product positioning.

metric_name: Windows and Unix/Linux treated as equivalents for open-systems storage
metric_value: Q4.2 and Q4.3 use identical structure; both excluded from mainframe category
notes: The parallel structure for Windows and Unix/Linux (with mainframe as a separate category) reflects the 2003 open-systems vs. proprietary divide. This was prescient: midline ATA storage adoption was almost entirely in the open-systems (Windows/Linux/Unix) category, consistent with the assumption embedded in Q4.

metric_name: Online disk vs. tape copy count
metric_value: Q5: Online disk copies (excluding original) / tape copies (each generation = one copy)
notes: Measuring total copy inventory (disk + tape) sizes the redundant storage overhead. If enterprises keep 2-3 tape generations plus 1-2 disk mirrors, the total redundant storage is 3-5x the original — midline storage as a copy target could cut cost of copies by 50-60%.

metric_name: Read-only / seldom-written data identification — refined framing
metric_value: Q6: Data that is effectively read-only or seldom written — closed transaction logs / old e-mail — but cannot yet be archived to tape
notes: V2 refines the cold-data concept from v1's "data that spins around" to "effectively read-only or seldom written." This more precise language maps directly to the data classification taxonomy that became standard in ILM: active (read/write) / reference (read-only) / archive (dormant). The Q6 category is the reference tier.

metric_name: Cold data application identification
metric_value: Q7: What applications fall under the read-only/seldom-written category? (Open-ended)
notes: Open-ended application identification reveals which specific enterprise applications were generating cold data in 2003. Expected answers: closed ERP transaction logs, old email, completed project files, historical databases. The actual answers would identify the specific use cases that MaXLine should target in marketing.

metric_name: Cold data percentage quantification
metric_value: Q8: What percent of total storage is the read-only/seldom-written data?
notes: Quantitative market sizing: what percentage of existing enterprise storage is cold data suitable for midline? This number directly determines the addressable market for MaXLine. If 20-40% of enterprise storage is cold (as hypothesized). that justified significant midline investment.

metric_name: Willingness-to-tradeoff — 1-7 Likert scale design
metric_value: Q9: Rate willingness to trade off on 3 scenarios (1=unwilling to tradeoff; 7=sign me up today)
notes: KEY INSTRUMENT INNOVATION: v2 replaces v1's binary choice with a 1-7 Likert scale across three scenarios. Scale anchor at 7: "sign me up today" — a vivid buyer-intent anchor. This scale enables statistical segmentation: scores 5-7 = early adopters; 3-4 = fence-sitters; 1-2 = resisters. The Likert design was far more analytically powerful than v1's binary.

metric_name: Willingness scenario A — highest value proposition
metric_value: Q9(a): Higher capacity + slightly less performance + slightly less availability + ~50% cost reduction
notes: Scenario A is the MaXLine sweet spot: maximum capacity, significant cost savings (50%), modest performance and availability concessions. This is the most aggressive midline proposition. If enterprises score 5-7 here, midline is commercially viable. If they score 1-3, the availability concession is a dealbreaker — enterprise storage must be nearly perfect.

metric_name: Willingness scenario B — availability-reduced conservative case
metric_value: Q9(b): Current disk sizes + current performance + slightly less availability + ~30% cost reduction
notes: Scenario B tests whether the availability concession (not the capacity increase) is the key barrier. If B scores higher than A, enterprises want cost savings but won't accept larger/slower disks. If A scores higher than B, capacity increase is acceptable and performance/availability concession is the actual issue.

metric_name: Willingness scenario C — no tradeoff required
metric_value: Q9(c): Current disk size + current performance + current availability + ~30% cost reduction
notes: Scenario C is the ideal buyer dream: 30% cheaper with no concessions. If C scores dramatically higher than A or B, it reveals price sensitivity without tolerance for any tradeoff — indicating midline must be presented as functionally equivalent, not lower-spec. The delta between C and A/B measures the cost of the performance/availability concession.

metric_name: Vendor engagement measurement
metric_value: Q10: Has your current storage supplier discussed low-cost disk options with you? (Supplier name?)
notes: Competitive intelligence embedded in a market research question: identifies which vendors were already having the midline conversation with enterprise accounts. If major vendors (EMC, HP, IBM) were already engaging on low-cost disk, the market education was underway; if not, Maxtor/Aberdeen had an opportunity to lead the conversation.

metric_name: Purchase intent — 1-7 scale
metric_value: Q11: If yes [vendor discussed low-cost options] — plans to purchase in next 12 months (1=Not at all; 7=Already have)
notes: Second 1-7 scale measures purchase intent for low-cost storage already being discussed. Scale anchor 7 = "already have" measures installed base; 5-6 = pipeline; 3-4 = consideration. This is a classic sales funnel measurement instrument embedded in market research — directly maps to modern Net Promoter Score and buying intent surveys.

metric_name: Restore frequency as reliability proxy
metric_value: Q12: How often do you restore one or more volumes from storage failure — weekly / monthly / quarterly / semi-annually / annually / less than annually
notes: Restore frequency measures storage reliability experience in practice. High restore frequency (weekly/monthly) indicates reliability is already a concern — these respondents may resist lower-availability midline. Low frequency (annually / rarely) indicates reliability is not a daily pain — these respondents are better midline candidates.

metric_name: Static data application identification — specific examples
metric_value: Q14: Single applications with very large non-volatile/static data — video data / images / data warehouse detailed / old e-mails
notes: Q14's explicit list (video / images / DW detailed data / old emails) is a remarkably accurate prediction of the primary midline storage use cases that proved commercially successful: medical imaging (PACS), video surveillance, data warehouse historical data, and email archiving were the four dominant midline ATA/SATA adoption categories by 2007-2010.

metric_name: Open-systems disk technology breakout — three types
metric_value: Q15: SCSI / FC / ATA percentage + drive sizes + rotational speeds — open-systems disk arrays only
notes: V2 scopes the disk technology question to open-systems only (Q15) — separate from mainframe (Q4.1). This is an important methodological refinement: mainframe disk (FICON-attached) was not relevant to midline positioning, and mixing it with open-systems data would have obscured the ATA baseline penetration.

metric_name: Backup/restore issues — five-symptom multi-select
metric_value: Q16: Five specific backup failure modes — offline window / job failure rate / cannot guarantee restore / management burden / restore time too long
notes: Five-symptom backup failure taxonomy captures the specific pain points that midline storage (as a backup target or as a destination for migrated cold data to reduce backup scope) could address. Each symptom maps to a midline benefit: (a) window = smaller backup set; (d) management burden = simplified tiered storage management; (e) restore time = SLA concern about midline restore speed.

metric_name: Terminology awareness — five-term battery
metric_value: Q17: Active archiving / online archiving / mezzanine storage / nearline storage / low-cost storage — all five tested
notes: V2 expands from v1's 3-term to 5-term terminology battery, adding 'online archiving' and 'low-cost storage.' The addition of 'low-cost storage' as a plain-language alternative is revealing: if respondents knew 'low-cost storage' but not 'nearline,' it would indicate the concept resonated but the branding was missing. This was indeed the market reality — 'nearline' later won as the standard term.

metric_name: Midline storage as complementary vs. substitute tier
metric_value: Q9's three scenarios implicitly test whether midline is a substitute (Scenario C) or complement (Scenarios A/B) for existing storage
notes: The survey design encodes a critical strategic hypothesis: if customers score Scenario A (less performance + less availability) higher than C (same as today just cheaper), they are buying a DIFFERENT tier, not just a cheaper version of the same thing. This is the complement vs. substitute positioning question that determined Maxtor's go-to-market strategy.

metric_name: Midline as complement vs. substitute — outcome
metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
notes: , Midline SATA proved to be a complementary tier (not a substitute for FC/SCSI) — enterprises used it for Tier 2/3 data while retaining high-performance FC for Tier 1. This was the dominant deployment model in EMC/HDS/IBM/NetApp midline SATA offerings by 2007-2010.

metric_name: 50% cost reduction target for maximum-tradeoff scenario
metric_value: Q9(a) states cost reduction ""on the order of 50%"" for higher-capacity / less-available option
notes: The 50% cost reduction target for Scenario A was realistic: ATA disk in 2003 cost approximately 50-60% less per GB than FC disk. By 2007, SATA cost 60-70% less than FC per GB. The 30% figure for Scenarios B and C was conservative — actual cost reductions were higher, making the real-world midline proposition MORE attractive than Scenario A implied.

metric_name: Unix / Linux / open-systems grouped as single category
metric_value: Q1 and Q4.3 treat Unix, Linux, and 'open systems' as a single category
notes: Grouping Unix, Linux, and open systems together in 2003 was pragmatically correct — Linux was still a minority in enterprise storage at the time. By 2008, Linux had surpassed Unix in new enterprise storage deployments, and the grouping became less accurate. However, for 2003 market sizing purposes, the combined category was appropriate.

metric_name: Question numbering gap — Q13 absent
metric_value: Survey jumps from Q12 to Q14 — Q13 missing or removed in v2 revision
notes: Q13 does not appear in the survey — numbering jumps from Q12 (restore frequency) to Q14 (static data apps). This suggests a question was removed during v1→v2 revision. Likely removed to reduce survey length or because the question was redundant with per-OS breakouts in Q4. The gap is a version artifact.

metric_name: Vendor name capture in Q10
metric_value: Q10 asks for supplier name when vendor has discussed low-cost disk — competitive intelligence design
notes: Capturing vendor names provides competitive intelligence: which storage vendors were already engaging customers on lower-cost disk? In 2003, EMC and HDS were beginning to position midline trays; Sun (StorEdge), HP (MSA), and IBM (DS4000/FAStT) were offering ATA options in some configurations. This competitive map was valuable to Maxtor for distribution channel strategy.

metric_name: Enterprise ATA adoption timing
metric_value: Survey deployed Q1 2003 — implies ATA adoption was at near-zero baseline in enterprise; survey measures inflection point readiness
notes: The entire survey design assumes enterprise ATA adoption was negligible (hence the willingness-to-adopt questions). This was accurate in early 2003. The subsequent SATA standard (ratified 2002; volume production 2004) triggered the adoption surge that the RAMP study was designed to predict. The instrument correctly measured the market just before the inflection.

metric_name: Enterprise ATA/SATA adoption trajectory — outcome
metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
notes: , By 2006 SATA drove 20-30% of new enterprise disk capacity; by 2010 SATA nearline was the dominant capacity-tier disk in most major enterprise arrays; by 2015 all-flash arrays began displacing SATA for performance tier while SATA held the capacity tier. Flash then displaced SATA for primary storage in most deployments by 2022.

metric_name: Per-OS max fill rate capture
metric_value: Q4.1(d) / Q4.2(d) / Q4.3(d): Maximum desired fill rate per OS platform
notes: Per-OS utilization ceiling measurement reveals whether Windows and Unix shops had different over-provisioning norms. Unix shops typically ran at 60-70% fill (conservative); Windows shops often higher but more variable. This per-OS granularity would identify whether midline's higher fill-rate tolerance had different uptake by platform.

metric_name: Online archiving vs. active archiving as distinct concepts
metric_value: Q17 tests both 'active archiving' and 'online archiving' as separate terms
notes: Distinguishing 'active archiving' (data actively managed and searchable in an archive tier) from 'online archiving' (simply archived but online) reflects the 2003 vocabulary ambiguity. The market eventually coalesced around 'active archive' for the access-capable tier and 'deep archive' for slow/cheap retrieval — consistent with this distinction.

metric_name: 7-point scale lower anchor set at complete unwillingness
metric_value: Q9 lower anchor 1 = 'unwilling to tradeoff' — not 'very unwilling' or 'strongly disagree'
notes: Setting anchor 1 = 'unwilling' (not 'strongly unwilling') compresses the negative end of the scale, which would shift mean scores upward and reduce apparent resistance. This is a deliberate or inadvertent design choice that would make midline receptivity appear slightly higher than a balanced scale. Methodological note for interpreting results.
